title: Shahi Tukda

ingredients.title: For Fried Bread
items: 8 slices white bread (slightly stale, crusts removed)

ingredients.title: For Rabri with Khoya
items: A few saffron strands, soaked in 2 tbsp warm milk
1 tbsp each chopped pistachios, almonds, and cashews

ingredients.title: Why Use or Skip Khoya in Shahi Tukda?
items: Rabri is slow-reduced milk that’s creamy and slightly runny, giving a light, rich texture that soaks well into fried bread.
Khoya is dense milk solids that make the dessert thicker, richer, and heavier.
Add 100-150g grated khoya to rabri near the end of cooking for a creamier, more indulgent dessert.
You can also fry khoya with sugar and cardamom and layer it with the bread for extra richness.

instruction.title: Fry the Bread
steps: Cut bread into triangles or rectangles.
Fry in hot ghee until golden and crisp. Drain on paper towels.

instruction.title: Prepare Sugar Syrup
steps: In a saucepan, combine sugar, water, cardamom, and saffron.
Heat until sugar dissolves, then simmer 3–4 minutes for a light syrup.
Add rose water if using. Keep warm.

instruction.title: Soak Bread
steps: Dip each fried bread slice briefly in the warm syrup (3–5 seconds per side) so it absorbs sweetness but stays intact.
Arrange on a serving platter.

instruction.title: Make Rabri with Khoya
steps: Boil milk in a heavy pan. Simmer on low, stirring often, until reduced by half.
Add sugar, cardamom powder, and saffron milk. Stir well.
Add grated khoya and cook 5–7 minutes until creamy but pourable.
Stir in most of the nuts (reserve some for garnish) and rose water if using. Remove from heat.

instruction.title: Assemble
steps: Pour warm rabri over the syrup-soaked bread slices.
Garnish with silver leaf, saffron strands, and remaining nuts.

instruction.title: Serve
steps: Best served slightly warm for a soft, royal texture, or chilled for a firmer bite.

instruction.title: To Keep Bread Crisp
steps: Use Slightly Stale Bread: Fresh bread is too soft and soggy, but slightly stale bread holds up better and crisps nicely when fried.
Remove Crusts: Crusts tend to become chewy and less absorbent, so trimming them off helps with even frying and syrup soaking.
Fry on Medium-High Heat: Fry the bread slices in hot ghee over medium-high heat until golden and crisp, not too dark. This creates a firm outer crust that resists sogginess.
Double Fry (Optional): For extra crispness, fry once, drain, then fry again briefly just before soaking in syrup.
Quick Syrup Dip: Dip the fried bread in warm syrup very briefly—3 to 5 seconds per side—so it absorbs just enough syrup without getting soggy.
Drain Immediately: After dipping, let the excess syrup drip off rather than soaking longer.
Serve Quickly: The longer soaked bread sits, the more it softens, so serve soon after assembling.
Thicker Slices: Cut bread slices slightly thicker (about 1 cm) to withstand the syrup without turning mushy.
Cool Frying Surface: After frying, place bread on wire racks instead of paper towels for better air circulation, which keeps them crisp.

chefTips: Rabri is slow-reduced milk that’s creamy and slightly runny, giving a light, rich texture that soaks well into fried bread.
Khoya is dense milk solids that make the dessert thicker, richer, and heavier.
Add 100-150g grated khoya to rabri near the end of cooking for a creamier, more indulgent dessert.
You can also fry khoya with sugar and cardamom and layer it with the bread for extra richness.
Use slightly stale bread and fry on medium-high heat for the best crisp texture.
